attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic reporting daily self-induced vomiting after meals for the past eight months. She acknowledges this behaviour is harmful but states she is not ready to stop. She has a BMI of 21 kg/m2. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 85/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. There is dental enamel erosion and calluses on her right hand. Serum electrolytes and renal function are within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate harm minimisation advice?
options: Recommend using ipecac syrup instead of manual induction
Rinse mouth with water immediately after vomiting
Brush teeth with toothpaste immediately after vomiting
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with ongoing purging behaviour and is not yet ready to engage in recovery-focused treatment. While the ultimate goal is to stop purging behaviours, harm minimisation strategies can help reduce immediate physical complications and maintain therapeutic engagement.

Rinsing the mouth with water immediately after vomiting is the most appropriate harm minimisation strategy. This helps neutralise and remove acidic gastric contents from the teeth, reducing dental erosion. The patient should wait at least 30 minutes before brushing teeth to avoid further damage to acid-softened enamel.

Brushing teeth with toothpaste immediately after vomiting can actually increase dental erosion by mechanically abrading the acid-softened enamel. This should be avoided until at least 30 minutes after purging.

Ipecac syrup is dangerous and can cause cardiac toxicity with repeated use. It should never be recommended as it is more harmful than manual induction of vomiting.

Using laxatives instead of vomiting is not appropriate harm minimisation advice as it can lead to different but equally serious complications including electrolyte disturbances, dehydration, and bowel dysfunction.

Taking vitamin supplements before purging does not prevent the physical complications of purging and may give a false sense of security about the behaviour's safety.

While providing harm minimisation advice, it is essential to simultaneously arrange appropriate mental health support and regularly monitor for medical complications. The patient should be informed about warning signs requiring urgent medical attention and encouraged to work towards stopping purging behaviours.
learningPoints: Medical complications of purging include electrolyte disturbances, dental erosion, and oesophageal damage.
Harm minimisation strategies in eating disorders aim to reduce physical complications while maintaining therapeutic engagement.
Harm minimisation advice should be given alongside referral for definitive treatment of the underlying disorder.

attributes.name: Level III
attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
